perturbation.original_text: The Parties hereby acknowledge and agree that, in the event of any breach of this Agreement by either Party, including, without limitation, the actual or threatened disclosure of a Party's Confidential Information without the express prior written consent of that Paity, such Party will suffer irreparable harm and injury and no remedy at law will afford it adequate protection against, or appropriate compensation for, such injury . Accordingly , each Party hereby agrees that, in any such event, the harmed Party shall be entitled to seek specific performance of the other Party's obligations under this Agreement , as well as such further injunctive relief as may be granted by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties hereby acknowledge and agree that, in the event of any breach of this Agreement by either Party, including, without limitation, the actual or threatened disclosure of a Party's Confidential Information without the express prior written consent of that Paity, the harmed party may seek monetary damages. Injunctive relief will not be pursued.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states that no remedy at law is adequate, and injunctive relief is available. Changing this to monetary damages only contradicts the original intent and limits the harmed party's options.
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah Code Ann. § 78B-6-201 generally allows for injunctive relief. By explicitly stating injunctive relief will not be pursued, the contract contradicts this general principle, which is relevant when irreparable harm is likely, as indicated in the original clause.
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows for specific performance and injunctive relief in the event of a breach, while the changed text explicitly states that injunctive relief will not be pursued, limiting the harmed party to monetary damages. According to the Law Explanation, Utah Code Ann. § 78B-6-201 generally allows for injunctive relief. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the existence of Utah Code Section 78B-6-201. By removing the option of injunctive relief, the changed contract contradicts the general principle of allowing such relief, particularly when irreparable harm is likely, as was acknowledged in the original text of the contract. This constitutes a direct contradiction of the law's provision for injunctive relief.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will continue in full force and effect until both parties agree in writing to terminate this agreement and for one -year thereafter. The Parties agree that if the negotiated transaction does not proceed within a reasonable time, or upon any request from either Party, each Party shall promptly deliver to the other Party all written Confidential Information and any other written material containing or reflecting Confidential Information and will not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part of such written material. All documents, memoranda, notes and other writings whatsoever prepared by each Party, or respective representatives, based on the information included in the Confidential Information shall be pro mptly destroyed, and such destruction shall be ce1tified in writing by an authorized officer supervising such destruction. Notwithstanding the return or destruction of any Confidential Information, each Party shall continue to be bound by its obligations o f confidentiality and other obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will continue in full force and effect until both parties agree in writing to terminate this agreement and for one -year thereafter. The Parties agree that if the negotiated transaction does not proceed within a reasonable time, or upon any request from either Party, each Party shall promptly deliver to the other Party all written Confidential Information and any other written material containing or reflecting Confidential Information and will not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part of such written material. All documents, memoranda, notes and other writings whatsoever prepared by each Party, or respective representatives, based on the information included in the Confidential Information shall be pro mptly destroyed. Notwithstanding the return or destruction of any Confidential Information, each Party shall continue to be bound by its obligations o f confidentiality and other obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the line about destruction certification, the process for ensuring the confidential data has been destroyed becomes open to interpretation, and no guarantees for destruction are provided.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Utah Code Ann. § 13-24-1 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the certification of destruction makes it harder to prove that reasonable steps were taken to protect trade secrets, which can be critical under the Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act (§ 13-24-3) when determining misappropriation.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly requires written certification of the destruction of confidential information. By removing this requirement, the changed text weakens the assurance and verifiability of the destruction process. The Law Explanation suggests this could make it harder to prove reasonable steps were taken to protect trade secrets under the Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act, though no snippet was provided to confirm the details of this particular act. Nevertheless, the removal of a documented destruction process creates a structural flaw that makes proving compliance more challenging.

perturbation.original_text: RELEASE. As consideration for being permitted by Kitty Haven to participate in activities and provide Services, I hereby agree that I, my assignees, heirs, distributees, guardians, and legal representatives will not make a claim against, sue, or attach the property of Kitty Haven Sanctuary of Utah for injury or damage resulting from any act, omission, negligence or other acts, howsoever caused, by any employee, agent, contractor, or representative of Kitty Haven as a result of my participation in activities and performance of the Services and any A -V Recordings. I here by release Kitty Haven from all actions, claims, or demands that I, my assignees, heirs, distributees, guardians, and legal representatives now have or may hereafter have for injury or damage resulting from my participation in activities and performance of the Services and any A -V Recordings.
perturbation.changed_text: RELEASE. As consideration for being permitted by Kitty Haven to participate in activities and provide Services, I hereby agree that I, my assignees, heirs, distributees, guardians, and legal representatives will not make a claim against, sue, or attach the property of Kitty Haven Sanctuary of Utah for injury or damage resulting from any act, omission, or other acts, howsoever caused, by any employee, agent, contractor, or representative of Kitty Haven as a result of my participation in activities and performance of the Services and any A -V Recordings. I here by release Kitty Haven from all actions, claims, or demands that I, my assignees, heirs, distributees, guardians, and legal representatives now have or may hereafter have for injury or damage resulting from my participation in activities and performance of the Services and any A -V Recordings.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the word 'negligence', the clause's effect is weakened. It might not protect Kitty Haven from negligence claims, particularly gross negligence. Under Utah Law, waivers for negligence must be clear and unequivocal.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Wagner v. Golf City, Inc., 26 Utah 2d 219, 487 P.2d 756 (1971)
perturbation.law_citation: Wagner v. Golf City, Inc., 26 Utah 2d 219, 487 P.2d 756 (1971)
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah case law, specifically Wagner v. Golf City, Inc., requires that exculpatory clauses clearly and unequivocally express the intent to release a party from liability for its own negligence. Removing the explicit reference to 'negligence' may render the release ambiguous and unenforceable regarding such claims.
perturbation.justification: The change removes the word 'negligence' from the release clause. The Law Explanation states that Utah case law (Wagner v. Golf City, Inc.) requires exculpatory clauses to clearly express the intent to release a party from liability for its own negligence. The Perturbation Explanation states that waivers for negligence under Utah law must be clear and unequivocal. The removal of the explicit term 'negligence' weakens the clause and may render it ambiguous and unenforceable regarding negligence claims. The modified text contradicts Utah law by failing to clearly release Kitty Haven from liability for its own negligence, potentially invalidating the release agreement.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and the rights and obligations of either Party under this Agreement may be assigned or transferred only upon the prior written approval of the other Party hereto. The rights and obligations of the Parties hereto will inure to the benefit of, will be binding upon, and will be enforceable by the Parties hereto, their representatives and their permitted successors and assigns.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and the rights and obligations of either Party under this Agreement may be assigned or transferred at will. The rights and obligations of the Parties hereto will inure to the benefit of, will be binding upon, and will be enforceable by the Parties hereto, their representatives and their permitted successors and assigns.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the assignment clause to 'at will' removes the requirement for prior written approval. This might be problematic if the Confidential Information is sensitive and the disclosing party would not want the risk to grow with an unknown entity.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 317 states that contractual rights are generally assignable unless the assignment is forbidden by statute or is otherwise inoperative on grounds of public policy, or the contract validly precludes assignment. Removing the requirement for prior approval changes the fundamental nature of the agreement, potentially creating risks if the receiving party is not someone the disclosing party would willingly share private information with.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: assignment | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you assignment Assignment is a legal term whereby an individual, the “assignor,” transfers rights, property, or other benefits to another, known as the “ assignee .” This concept is used in both contract and property law. The term can refer to either the act of transfer or the rights /property/benefits being transferred. Contract Law Under contract law, assignment of a contract is both an assignment of rights and a delegation of duties in the absence of evidence otherwise. For example, if A contracts with B to teach B guitar for $50, A can assign this contract to C. That is, this assignment is both an assignment of A’s rights under the contract to the $50; and a delegation of A’s duty to teach guitar to C. In this example, A is both the “assignor” and the “delegee” who delegates the duties to another (C), C is known as the “ obligor ” who must perform the obligations to the assignee, and B is the “assignee” who is owed duties and is liable to the “obligor.” Assignment of Rights/Duties Under Contract Law There are a few notable rules regarding assignments under contract law. First, if an individual has not yet secured the contract to perform duties to another, he/she cannot assign his/her future right to an assignee. That is, if A has not yet contracted with B to teach B guitar, A cannot assign his/her rights to C. Second, rights cannot be assigned when they materially change the obligor’s duty and rights. Third, the obligor can sue the assignee directly if the assignee does not pay them. Following the previous example, this means that C (obligor) can sue B (assignee) if C teaches guitar to B, but B does not pay C $50 in return. Delegation of Duties If the promised performance requires a rare genius or skill, then the delegee cannot delegate it to the obligor. It can only be delegated if the promised performance is more commonplace. Further, an obligee can sue if the assignee does not perform. However, the delegee is secondarily liable unless there has been an express release of the delegee. That is, if B does want C to teach guitar but C refuses to, then B can sue C. If C still refuses to perform, then B can compel A to fulfill the duties under secondary liability. Lastly, a related concept is novation , which is when a new obligor substitutes and releases an old obligor. If novation occurs, then the original obligor’s duties are wiped out. However, novation requires an original obligee’s consent . Property Law Under property law, assignment typically arises in landlord-tenant situations. For example, A might be renting from landlord B but wants another party (C) to take over the property. In this scenario, A might be able to choose between assigning and subleasing the property to C. If assigning, A would be giving the entire balance of the term to C, with no reversion to anyone; whereas if subleasing, A would be giving the balance to C for a limited period of the remaining term. Under assignment, C would have privity of estate with the landlord, while under a sublease C would not. [Last reviewed in January of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE PROPERTY landlord & tenant property & real estate law trusts inheritances & estates business law contracts wex definitions Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The original text requires prior written approval for assignment or transfer, while the changed text allows assignment or transfer 'at will'. This directly contradicts the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 317, as cited in the Law Explanation, which states that contractual rights are generally assignable unless the contract validly precludes assignment. By removing the prior approval requirement, the changed text eliminates a contractual restriction on assignment, thereby creating a risk that sensitive information could be shared with an unknown entity which the original disclosing party would not agree to.
